segments.s023-g17: به + NP + verb: the preposition به ('to/towards/into') introduces the goal 'ایوان ضحاک' (Zahhāk’s iwan); the finite verb بردندشان is past plural بردند ('they took') with the enclitic object pronoun -شان 'them' attached. Word order is typical Persian narrative: prepositional phrase before the verb, verb at the end.
segments.s023-g18: بر آن اژدهافش is a prepositional phrase ('upon/onto that dragon-like one') where آن is the demonstrative modifying the compound epithet اژدهافش. سپردندشان is past plural سپردند ('they handed/entrusted') with the same enclitic -شان 'them'. The clause therefore marks transfer of the captives to the dragon-figure.
segments.s023-g19: بپروردشان is a classical/epic-form verb with the prefix بـ plus the stem پرورد and the object enclitic -شان, functioning here as a third-person action 'he raised them.' از ره جادویی is an ablative/means phrase ('by/through a magical way'), where از marks the means and ره جادویی is the noun phrase 'magical way.'
segments.s023-g20: بیاموختشان is similarly formed (بـ + آموخت + -شان) and means 'he taught them' in this narrative. The direct objects follow without a preposition: کژی و بدخویی list the moral vices taught ('crookedness' and 'ill-temper'), joined by و.
segments.s023-g21: ندانست is the negative past of دانستن ('he knew not' / 'he did not know'). جز introduces the exception; here کژی آموختن is an infinitival phrase ('to teach crookedness') functioning as the sole thing he knew/was able to do. Note آموختن is the infinitive 'to teach' (not 'to learn') in this usage.
segments.s023-g22: This second جز + از phrase ('except from/other than') introduces a list of activities that are the content of that exception: کشتن, غارت, و سوختن are verbal nouns/infinitives meaning 'killing, plunder, and burning', linked by و. The structure emphasizes that nothing beyond violent deeds was known or taught.
segments.s023-g1: چو is a temporal subordinating particle meaning “when” (introducing a clause); ضحاک is the proper name Zahhak as subject; شد is the past copular/verb ‘became’; بر + جهان marks the scope ‘over the world’ with بر as a preposition before its object جهان; شهریار is a predicate noun 'ruler/king' placed after the prepositional phrase (poetic word order).
segments.s023-g2: بر او is a dative/prepositional phrase ‘upon him’ (او = ‘him’); سالیان انجمن شد هزار is a somewhat inverted, poetic clause: انجمن شد = ‘assemblies/councils came to pass/occurred’ and هزار (placed after the verb) supplies the numeral ‘a thousand’; overall: ‘a thousand assemblies/years befell him.’
segments.s023-g3: سراسر زمانه is the noun phrase ‘the whole/entire age’; بدو is a dative phrase ‘toward him’ (to him); گشت (past of گشتن) here means ‘turned’ or ‘was turned’ and باز is an adverb ‘again/back’ modifying the verb — together: ‘the whole age turned back to him.’
segments.s023-g4: برآمد is an intransitive past verb ‘arose/occurred’ (i.e. a period began); بر این روزگار دراز is a nominal phrase ‘this long time/era’ with بر here giving the sense ‘upon/in the form of’ or simply linking the verb to the temporal phrase — literal: ‘this long time arose.’
segments.s023-g5: نهان گشت is a verb phrase ‘became hidden’ (نهان as predicate/adverb + گشت ‘became/turned’); کردار فرزانگان is a genitive construction ‘the deeds/conduct of the sages’ (فرزانگان = ‘wise ones’). Word order places the predicate before the possessed noun phrase, a poetic inversion.
segments.s023-g6: پراگنده شد is ‘became widespread/scattered’ (پراگنده + شد); کام دیوانگان is the possessed phrase ‘the desire(s) of the madmen’ (کام = desire, دیوانگان = madmen). Together: ‘the madmen’s desires became widespread/ran riot.’
segments.s023-g7: هنر خوار شد is a short clause: هنر (art/skill/virtue) is subject and خوار شد is a passive construction ‘was despised/held cheap’ (خوار = despised, شد = became/was); the following pair جادویی ارجمند is a noun + adjective ‘magical (or magic) — noble/esteemed’ where the copula is elliptical (poetry often omits an explicit verb, implying ‘was valued/esteemed’).
segments.s023-g8: نهان راستی is ‘truth hidden’ (نهان modifying or predicated of راستی ‘truth’); آشکارا is an adverb ‘openly/evidently’ modifying the noun گزند (harm/damage), with an omitted copula so that the sense is ‘truth was hidden, and harm was openly apparent/caused.’ This juxtaposition (noun + adjective/adverb) is common in classical poetic diction.
segments.s023-g9: شده is a past participle/auxiliary meaning 'has become' (perfective result). The phrase بر بدی دست دیوان دراز is a predicative clause: بر = 'toward' (direction), بدی = 'evil' (abstract object of that direction), دست = 'hand' used figuratively for power or reach, دیوان here refers to the ruling court/office (metonymic for those in power), and دراز is the adjective 'long' that follows and completes the predicate. Word order is typical Persian: subject/topic (دست دیوان) + predicate adjective (دراز) with شده marking the resultant state.
segments.s023-g10: سخن is the subject 'speech' or 'what was said'. نرفتی is the past form with a negative prefix ن- meaning 'did not go/turn', so به نیکی نرفتی literally 'did not go towards goodness' where به = 'toward' and نیکی = 'goodness'. جز به راز is an exception phrase: جز = 'except', به here functions as 'in/by' in the idiom جز به راز = 'except in secret.' Overall: 'speech did not turn to goodness except in secret.'
segments.s023-g11: دو پاکیزه is a noun phrase 'two chaste (ones)', where پاکیزه is an adjective meaning 'chaste' (here describing persons). از خانه جمشید marks source: از = 'from', خانه = 'house', جمشید = proper name (Jamshid). Word order places the numeral+adjective before the source phrase.
segments.s023-g12: آوریدند is the past-tense transitive verb 'they brought/they carried (out)'; برون is an adverb 'out/forth' modifying it. لرزان is a predicative/adjectival participle 'trembling' describing the brought women; چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and بید is the simile image 'a willow'. The clause order is verb (they brought out) followed by descriptive phrase (trembling like a willow).
segments.s023-g13: که here introduces or links the explanatory clause ('for/that'). جمشید را uses را as a dative/possessive marker 'to Jamshid' (Jamshid as possessor). هر دو دختر is 'both daughters' (هر emphasizing the two), and بدند is an archaic third-person plural past verb functioning as a copula/verb meaning 'were' or 'belonged (to)'. So the clause says that both belonged to Jamshid (were his daughters).
segments.s023-g14: سر بانوان را is 'the heads of the ladies' with را marking the definite object/topic. چو = 'like/as' introduces the comparison, افسر here means 'helmet' (a head‑covering), and بدند again is the same archaic past verb; in this context it is used transitively/causatively in sense 'they put/placed (on)' or 'were set' (poetic usage). Syntactically this yields 'they set/placed on the ladies' heads (something) like a helmet.'
segments.s023-g15: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/of'. پوشیده‌رویان is a plural noun 'the veiled-faced (women)' or 'those of concealed faces', one of whom is then named. یکی introduces 'one' (of them) and شهرناز is a proper name (Shahernaz). The phrase literally: 'from the veiled-faced, one [is] Shahernaz.'
segments.s023-g16: دگر = 'the other/another' marks the second of the two. پاکدامن is an adjective/noun 'chaste/upright one' describing her. به نام is an idiom 'by the name (of)' with به functioning as the prepositional element in naming, نام = 'name', and ارنواز is the proper name (Arnavaz). The phrase: 'the other chaste, by the name Arnavaz.'

sentences.s023-s9: They led them into Zahhāk’s iwan and handed them over to that dragon-like one.
sentences.s023-s10: He raised them by a magical means and taught them crookedness and ill temper.
sentences.s023-s11: He knew nothing except to teach crookedness — nothing but killing, plunder, and burning.
sentences.s023-s1: When Zahhak became ruler of the world, a thousand assemblies (years) came upon him.
sentences.s023-s2: The whole age again turned toward him; this long era arose.
sentences.s023-s3: The deeds of the wise were hidden; the desires of the madmen spread far and wide.
sentences.s023-s4: Skill was despised; noble magic was valued; truth was hidden and harm was made manifest openly.
sentences.s023-s5: The court's hand grew long in evil; speech did not turn to goodness except in secret.
sentences.s023-s6: They brought out two chaste (maidens) from Jamshid's house, trembling like a willow.
sentences.s023-s7: For both were daughters of Jamshid; they set (on) the ladies' heads like helmets.
sentences.s023-s8: Of the veiled-faced (women), one was Shahernaz, the other chaste, named Arnavaz.
